revision.text: #redirect[[en:Yamashina_family]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''山科家'''（'''やましなけ'''）は、[[羽林家]]の家格を有する[[公家]]。[[藤原北家]][[四条流]]。家名は[[京都]][[山科]]に由来する。家業は[[装束]]・[[衣紋]]で[[江戸時代]]には[[高倉家]]（[[藪家]]）とともに装束色目を担当した。江戸時代の家禄は300石。
[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された。[[戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。
[[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。 {{history-stub}} [[category:藤原氏|やましなけ]] [[category:公家|やましなけ]] [[Category:日本の氏族|やましなけ]]

[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。
[[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。
{{history-stub}} [[category:藤原氏|やましなけ]] [[category:公家|やましなけ]] [[Category:日本の氏族|やましなけ]]

'''山科家'''（やましなけ）は、[[羽林家]]の家格を有する[[公家]]。[[藤原北家]][[四条流]]。家名は[[京都]][[山科]]に由来する。家業は[[装束]]・[[衣紋]]で[[江戸時代]]には[[高倉家]]（[[藪家]]）とともに装束色目を担当した。江戸時代の家禄は300石。
[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。

'''山科家'''（やましなけ）は、[[羽林家]]の家格を有する[[公家]]。[[藤原北家]][[四条流]]。家名は[[京都]][[山科]]に由来する。家業は[[装束]]・[[衣紋]]で[[江戸時代]]には[[高倉家]]（[[藪家]]）とともに装束色目を担当した。江戸時代の家禄は300石。
[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。言継の息子言経は、勅勘をこうむり摂津国に下ったため、一族の教利が一時期、山科を名乗る、しかし、徳川家康の意向により、言経が朝廷に複帰したため、教利は、猪熊教利と名を変えざるをえなくなった。 [[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。
{{japanese-history-stub|やましなけ}}
[[category:藤原氏|+やましなけ]] [[category:公家|やましなけ]]

[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。言継の息子言経は、勅勘をこうむり摂津国に下ったため、一族の教利が一時期、山科を名乗り朝廷に仕える、しかし、徳川家康の意向により、言経が朝廷に複帰したため、教利は、猪熊教利と名を変えざるをえなくなった。

'''山科家'''（やましなけ）は、[[羽林家]]の家格を有する[[公家]]。[[藤原北家]][[四条流]]。家名は家領があった[[京都]][[山科|山科荘]]に由来する。家業は[[装束]]・[[衣紋]]で[[江戸時代]]には[[高倉家]]（[[藪家]]）とともに装束色目を担当した。江戸時代の家禄は300石。
[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された。[[南北朝時代_(日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[山科教言]]以後、代々[[内蔵頭]]を輩出して朝廷財政を運営した。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。言継の息子言経は、勅勘をこうむり摂津国に下ったため、一族の[[猪熊教利|教利]]が一時期、山科を名乗り朝廷に仕える、しかし、徳川家康の意向により、言経が朝廷に複帰したため、教利は、猪熊教利と名を変えざるをえなくなった。
[[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。
== 参考文献 == * 菅原正子『中世公家の経済と文化』（吉川弘文館、1998年） ISBN 4642027629 : 中世山科家の家領経営と家業。
[[Category:藤原氏|+やましなけ]] [[Category:公家|やましなけ]] {{japanese-history-stub|やましなけ}}

revision.comment: 祖は教成（『戦国時代の貴族』参照）
revision.text:
'''山科家'''（やましなけ）は、[[羽林家]]の家格を有する[[公家]]。[[藤原北家]][[四条流]]。家名は家領があった[[京都]][[山科|山科荘]]に由来する。家業は[[装束]]・[[衣紋]]で[[江戸時代]]には[[高倉家]]（[[藪家]]）とともに装束色目を担当した。江戸時代の家禄は300石。
[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]の猶子となった[[中納言|権中納言]][[藤原教成|教成]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された。[[南北朝時代_(日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[山科教言]]以後、代々[[内蔵頭]]を輩出して朝廷財政を運営した。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。言継の息子言経は、勅勘をこうむり摂津国に下ったため、一族の[[猪熊教利|教利]]が一時期、山科を名乗り朝廷に仕える、しかし、徳川家康の意向により、言経が朝廷に複帰したため、教利は、猪熊教利と名を変えざるをえなくなった。
[[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。
== 参考文献 == * [[今谷明]]『戦国時代の貴族 <small>「言継卿記」が描く京都</small>』（講談社学術文庫、2002年） ISBN 4061595350 * 菅原正子『中世公家の経済と文化』（吉川弘文館、1998年） ISBN 4642027629 : 中世山科家の家領経営と家業。
[[Category:藤原氏|+やましなけ]] [[Category:公家|やましなけ]] {{japanese-history-stub|やましなけ}}

[[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。

{{日本の氏族|家名=山科家|家紋名称=折り入り菱|本姓=[[藤原北家]][[四条家|四条流]][[庶家|庶流]]|家祖=[[藤原教成]]|種別=[[公家]]（[[羽林家]]）|出身地=[[京都]]|所領地= |人物=[[山科言継]]|支流=[[猪熊家]]（[[公家]]）}}
'''山科家'''（やましなけ）は、[[羽林家]]の家格を有する[[公家]]。[[藤原北家]][[四条流]]。家名は家領があった[[京都]][[山科|山科荘]]に由来する。家業は[[装束]]・[[衣紋]]で[[江戸時代]]には[[高倉家]]（[[藪家]]）とともに装束色目を担当した。[[江戸時代]]の家禄は300石。
[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]の猶子となった[[中納言|権中納言]][[藤原教成|教成]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された<ref>『戦国時代の貴族 <small>「言継卿記」が描く京都</small>』、[[今谷明]]、講談社、2002、p.13</ref>。[[南北朝時代_(日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[山科教言]]以後、代々[[内蔵頭]]を輩出して朝廷財政を運営した。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。言継の息子言経は、勅勘をこうむり[[摂津国]]に下ったため、一族の[[猪熊教利|教利]]が一時期、山科を名乗り[[朝廷]]に仕える、しかし、[[徳川家康]]の意向により、言経が[[朝廷]]に複帰したため、教利は、猪熊教利と名を変えざるをえなくなった。
[[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。

== 創立から明治までの動向 == [[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]の[[猶子]]となった[[中納言|権中納言]][[藤原教成|教成]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された今谷明『戦国時代の貴族 「言継卿記」が描く京都』 p13。[[南北朝時代_(日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[山科教言]]以後、代々[[内蔵頭]]を輩出して朝廷財政を運営した。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。言継の息子言経は、勅勘をこうむり[[摂津国]]に下ったため、一族の[[猪熊教利|教利]]が一時期、山科を名乗り[[朝廷]]に仕える、しかし、[[徳川家康]]の意向により、言経が[[朝廷]]に複帰したため、教利は、猪熊教利と名を変えざるをえなくなった。 == 明治以降から現在までの動向 == [[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。
[[冷泉家]]とともに、維新後も京都に本拠を置き続けている数少ない旧公家である。 == 参考文献 == * [[今谷明]]『戦国時代の貴族 「言継卿記」が描く京都』（[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[2002年]]） ISBN 4-06-159535-0 * 菅原正子『中世公家の経済と文化』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[1998年]]） ISBN 4-642-02762-9 : 中世山科家の家領経営と家業。 == 脚注 == [[Category:山科家|*]] [[Category:公家|やましなけ]] {{japanese-history-stub|やましなけ}}

[[若王子家]]（[[奈良華族]]） }} '''山科家'''（やましなけ）は、[[羽林家]]の家格を有する[[公家]]。[[藤原北家]][[四条流]]。家名は家領があった[[京都]][[山科|山科荘]]に由来する。　家業は[[装束]]・[[衣紋]]で[[江戸時代]]には[[高倉家]]（[[藪家]]）とともに装束色目を担当した。[[江戸時代]]の家禄は300石。 __TOC__
== 創立から明治までの動向 == [[四条家]]の[[四条家成]]の六男[[藤原実教|実教]]の子（[[猶子]]）[[中納言|権中納言]][[藤原教成|教成]]を祖として[[平安時代]]末期に創設された今谷明『戦国時代の貴族 「言継卿記」が描く京都』 p13。[[南北朝時代_(日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[山科教言]]以後、代々[[内蔵頭]]を輩出して朝廷財政を運営した。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[山科言継|言継]]（ときつぐ）は[[日記]]『[[言継卿記]]』を著した。『言継卿記』は、戦国時代の[[京都]]を中心とする[[畿内]]の情勢を知る上で必要不可欠な一級史料として知られる。言継の息子言経は、勅勘をこうむり[[摂津国]]に下ったため、一族の[[猪熊教利|教利]]が一時期、山科を名乗り[[朝廷]]に仕える、しかし、[[徳川家康]]の意向により、言経が[[朝廷]]に複帰したため、教利は、猪熊教利と名を変えざるをえなくなった。 ==　系図　== 四条実教-山科教成-教房-資成-資行-教行-教繁-教遠-教言-教興-教豊-顕言-保宗-言国-定言-言綱-[[山科言継|言継]]-[[山科言経|言経]]-言緒-言総-言行-持言-堯言-頼言-敬言-忠言-言知-言成-言縄 == 明治以降から現在までの動向 == [[明治維新]]後、[[山科言縄|言縄]]（ときなお）の時に[[伯爵]]位を賜る。
[[冷泉家|上冷泉家]]とともに、維新後も[[京都]]に本拠を置き続けている数少ない旧公家である。 == 参考文献 == * [[今谷明]]『戦国時代の貴族 「言継卿記」が描く京都』（[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[2002年]]） ISBN 4-06-159535-0 * 菅原正子『中世公家の経済と文化』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[1998年]]） ISBN 4-642-02762-9 : 中世山科家の家領経営と家業。 == 脚注 == [[Category:山科家|*]] {{japanese-history-stub|やましなけ}}

{{日本の氏族 |家名=山科家 |家紋名称=折り入り菱 |本姓=[[藤原北家]][[四条家|四条流]][[庶家|庶流]] |家祖=[[藤原教成]] |種別=[[公家]]（[[羽林家]]）
[[華族]]（[[伯爵]]） |出身地=[[山城国]] |根拠地=[[山城国]][[山科区|山科荘]]

[[杉渓家]]（[[華族]]（[[男爵]]））

[[冷泉家|上冷泉家]]とともに、維新後も[[京都]]に本拠を置き続けている数少ない旧公家である。 == 参考文献 == * [[今谷明]]『戦国時代の貴族 「言継卿記」が描く京都』（[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[2002年]]） ISBN 4-06-159535-0 * 菅原正子『中世公家の経済と文化』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[1998年]]） ISBN 4-642-02762-9 : 中世山科家の家領経営と家業。 == 脚注 == {{DEFAULTSORT:やましなけ}} [[Category:山科家|*]] [[category:日本貴族の家系]] {{japanese-history-stub|やましなけ}}

Created page with "{{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [..."
adding original pages again becqause I missed many
adding new pages tag because that is one I use for finding new links
Moving from [[Category:New pages]] to [[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Yamashina Clan]] [[Category:pages As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, {{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yamashina family}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yamashina family}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Original pages]] to [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yamashina family}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion revision.text:
{{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORTYamashina family}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yamashina family}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] {{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yamashina {{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{citation needed|Yamashina revision.text:
{{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Period === The clan traces its origins to [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni]], the sixth son of [[Fujiwara no Ienaga|藤原家成]], a court noble from the late {{ill|Heian Period|ja|平安時代}}. The clan's name derives from its ancestral estates in [[Yamashina|山科]], Kyoto. Initially, it was known as the Reizei clan but later adopted the Yamashina name during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}.{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} The Yamashina clan specialized in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly ceremonial attire and court music (especially {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}}).{{sfn|Hashimoto, Masanobu|2010|p=582}} ... == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[ja:山科家]]

{{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家}} rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言}} with the court rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}} rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}} rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == {{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家}} rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言}} with the court rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}} rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == {{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家}} rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言}} with the court rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}} rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Yamashina family]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Default {{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家}} rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言}} with the court rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}} rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == {{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)

[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. {{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家}} rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言}} with the court rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}} rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} [[Category:Yamashina family]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yamashina family}} [[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}} rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == [[ja:山科家]]

{{Japanese Clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|山城国]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial House)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家}}, its rank was {{ill|Baronial House|ja|羽林家}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|公卿}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家}} rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言}} with the court rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}} rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. ==
