[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court noble|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|qq=}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|qq=}}, its Rank was {{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Count|qq=}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sanenori|qq=}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ienari|qq=}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|qq=}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūshi|qq=}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokudaiji Kimitada|qq=}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina District, Kyoto|qq=}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Noriyuki|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke|qq=}} Rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchino|qq=}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku kojitsu|qq=}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushoku|qq=}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō|qq=}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norishige|qq=}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Narihira|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashina no Eishi|qq=}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the Rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} with the court Rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō-ni-i|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imatani Akira|qq=}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina East Manor|qq=}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anidera Manor|qq=}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Manor|qq=}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-Ibo Manor|qq=}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzu Manor|qq=}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuta Manor|qq=}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azabu Manor|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikagedō|qq=}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norimoto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurō-donari|qq=}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzoku|qq=}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emon-dō|qq=}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō family|qq=}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haihan-chiken|qq=}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinroku Kōshō|qq=}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, Ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peerage Act (Japan)|qq=}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokinao|qq=}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakushaku|qq=}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainagon|qq=}} Rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokikazu|qq=}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} {{translated page|ja|山科家|version=103722627|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Yamashina family]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Yamashina Clan]] [[Category:Baronial Houses]] [[Category:Japanese Count Houses]] }} [[sn:Yamashina family]] [[en:Yamashina family]] [[sn:amashina family]] [[ja:山科家]]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]]<!--Doing this to bump these pages because my history retrieval attempts have led to category caching errors-->[[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Japanese clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Houses|qq=}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Count|qq=}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial Houses)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court noble|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|qq=}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|qq=}}, its Rank was {{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Count|qq=}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sanenori|qq=}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ienari|qq=}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|qq=}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūshi|qq=}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokudaiji Kimitada|qq=}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina District, Kyoto|qq=}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Noriyuki|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke|qq=}} Rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchino|qq=}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku kojitsu|qq=}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushoku|qq=}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō|qq=}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norishige|qq=}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Narihira|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashina no Eishi|qq=}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the Rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} with the court Rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō-ni-i|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imatani Akira|qq=}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina East Manor|qq=}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anidera Manor|qq=}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Manor|qq=}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-Ibo Manor|qq=}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzu Manor|qq=}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuta Manor|qq=}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azabu Manor|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikagedō|qq=}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norimoto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurō-donari|qq=}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzoku|qq=}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emon-dō|qq=}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō family|qq=}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haihan-chiken|qq=}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinroku Kōshō|qq=}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, Ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peerage Act (Japan)|qq=}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokinao|qq=}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakushaku|qq=}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainagon|qq=}} Rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokikazu|qq=}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} {{translated page|ja|山科家|version=103722627|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Yamashina family]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Yamashina Clan]] [[Category:Baronial Houses]] [[Category:Japanese Count Houses]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamashina family]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Yamashina family]] [[en:Yamashina family]] [[sn:amashina family]] [[ja:山科家]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Japanese Count Houses]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Japanese clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Houses|qq=}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Count|qq=}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial Houses)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court noble|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|qq=}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|qq=}}, its Rank was {{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Count|qq=}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sanenori|qq=}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ienari|qq=}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|qq=}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūshi|qq=}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokudaiji Kimitada|qq=}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina District, Kyoto|qq=}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Noriyuki|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke|qq=}} Rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchino|qq=}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku kojitsu|qq=}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushoku|qq=}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō|qq=}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norishige|qq=}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Narihira|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashina no Eishi|qq=}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the Rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} with the court Rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō-ni-i|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imatani Akira|qq=}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina East Manor|qq=}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anidera Manor|qq=}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Manor|qq=}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-Ibo Manor|qq=}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzu Manor|qq=}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuta Manor|qq=}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azabu Manor|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikagedō|qq=}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norimoto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurō-donari|qq=}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzoku|qq=}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emon-dō|qq=}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō family|qq=}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haihan-chiken|qq=}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinroku Kōshō|qq=}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, Ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peerage Act (Japan)|qq=}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokinao|qq=}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakushaku|qq=}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainagon|qq=}} Rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokikazu|qq=}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} {{translated page|ja|山科家|version=103722627|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Yamashina family]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Yamashina Clan]] [[Category:Baronial Houses]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamashina family]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Yamashina family]] [[en:Yamashina family]] [[sn:amashina family]] [[ja:山科家]]

The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke|qq=}} Rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchino|qq=}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku kojitsu|qq=}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushoku|qq=}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō|qq=}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norishige|qq=}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Narihira|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashina no Eishi|qq=}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the Rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} with the court Rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō-ni-i|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imatani Akira|qq=}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina East Manor|qq=}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anidera Manor|qq=}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Manor|qq=}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-Ibo Manor|qq=}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzu Manor|qq=}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuta Manor|qq=}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azabu Manor|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikagedō|qq=}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norimoto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurō-donari|qq=}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzoku|qq=}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emon-dō|qq=}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō family|qq=}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haihan-chiken|qq=}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinroku Kōshō|qq=}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, Ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peerage Act (Japan)|qq=}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokinao|qq=}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakushaku|qq=}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainagon|qq=}} Rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokikazu|qq=}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} {{translated page|ja|山科家|version=103722627|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Yamashina family]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Baronial Houses]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamashina family]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Yamashina family]] [[en:Yamashina family]] [[sn:amashina family]] [[ja:山科家]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Japanese clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Houses|qq=}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ({{ill|Count|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Count|qq=}}) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial Houses)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a {{ill|Japanese court noble|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court noble|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|qq=}} and later a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} family. As a {{ill|Kuge|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuge|qq=}}, its Rank was {{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, and as a {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}}, its title was that of a {{ill|Count|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Count|qq=}}.{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sanenori|qq=}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ienari|qq=}}, a {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|qq=}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūshi|qq=}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokudaiji Kimitada|qq=}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in {{ill|Yamashina District, Kyoto|ja|山科区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina District, Kyoto|qq=}}, but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-{{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}} under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Noriyuki|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke|qq=}} Rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchino|qq=}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku kojitsu|qq=}}, particularly in {{ill|Fukushoku|ja|服飾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushoku|qq=}} (court attire) and {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, specializing in the {{ill|Shō|ja|笙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō|qq=}} (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norishige|qq=}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Narihira|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashina no Eishi|qq=}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the Rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} with the court Rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō-ni-i|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imatani Akira|qq=}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina East Manor|qq=}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anidera Manor|qq=}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Manor|qq=}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-Ibo Manor|qq=}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzu Manor|qq=}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuta Manor|qq=}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azabu Manor|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikagedō|qq=}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norimoto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurō-donari|qq=}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzoku|qq=}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emon-dō|qq=}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō family|qq=}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in {{ill|Gagaku|ja|雅楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gagaku|qq=}}, particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku|qq=}} peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the {{ill|Haihan-chiken|ja|廃藩置県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haihan-chiken|qq=}}, the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinroku Kōshō|qq=}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, Ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peerage Act (Japan)|qq=}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokinao|qq=}} was granted the title of {{ill|Hakushaku|ja|伯爵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakushaku|qq=}} (Count) due to his family's history of holding the {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainagon|qq=}} Rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokikazu|qq=}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} {{translated page|ja|山科家|version=103722627|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Baronial Houses]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamashina family]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Yamashina family]] [[en:Yamashina family]] [[sn:amashina family]] [[ja:山科家]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Japanese clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Houses|qq=}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ([[:en:Count|Count]]) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial Houses)
[[Ikoma Clan|生駒氏]] (Warrior Clan)
[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)
[[Wakaōji Clan|若王子家]] (Barony) }} '''Yamashina Clan''' (山科家) was a [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and later a [[:en:Kazoku|Kazoku]] family. As a [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]], its Rank was {{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, and as a [[:en:Kazoku|Kazoku]], its title was that of a [[:en:Count|Count]].{{sfn|Odabe, Yūji|2006|p=326}} == History == === Feudal Era === The Yamashina family traces its origins to {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanenori|ja|藤原実教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sanenori|qq=}}, the sixth son of the late {{ill|Fujiwara no Ienari|ja|藤原家成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ienari|qq=}}, a [[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]] and court noble ({{ill|Kugyō|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kugyō|qq=}}) of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūshi|qq=}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokudaiji Kimitada|qq=}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The name "Yamashina" derives from the family's estate in [[:en:Yamashina-ku, Kyoto|Yamashina District, Kyoto]], but originally, they used the name "Reizei" and only adopted "Yamashina" as their family name during the mid-[[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] under the leadership of {{ill|Yamashina Noriyuki|ja|山科教行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Noriyuki|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. The family belonged to the {{ill|Buke|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke|qq=}} Rank among the court nobility and held the {{ill|Uchino|ja|内々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchino|qq=}} status{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Their traditional roles included expertise in {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku kojitsu|qq=}}, particularly in [[:en:Costume|Fukushoku]] (court attire) and [[:en:Gagaku|Gagaku]], specializing in the [[:en:Shō (instrument)|Shō]] (mouth organ){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. {{ill|Yamashina Norishige|ja|山科教成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norishige|qq=}}, a close retainer of {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}}, became the adopted son of Sanenori and succeeded him. Norishige was the son of {{ill|Taira no Narihira|ja|平業房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Narihira|qq=}} and {{ill|Takashina no Eishi|ja|高階栄子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashina no Eishi|qq=}} (Tango no Tsubone), a favored consort of the Retired Emperor. Due to his mother's influence, Emperor Go-Shirakawa arranged for Norishige to be adopted by Fujiwara no Sanenori{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Norishige eventually rose to the Rank of {{ill|Gon-Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|1=Chūnagon|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-Chūnagon|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūnagon|qq=}} with the court Rank of {{ill|Shō-ni-i|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō-ni-i|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}}{{ill|Imatani Akira|ja|今谷明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imatani Akira|qq=}}, *The Aristocrats of the Sengoku Period: The World of *Tokitsugu Kyōki*.*, p. 13. Norishige inherited all the estates granted to his mother by Emperor Go-Shirakawa, which became the core Yamashina family holdings. These included estates in {{ill|Yamashina East Manor|ja|山科東荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina East Manor|qq=}} (Yamashiro Province), {{ill|Anidera Manor|ja|尼寺荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anidera Manor|qq=}} (Mino Province), {{ill|Sumiyoshi Manor|ja|住吉荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Manor|qq=}} (Shinano Province), {{ill|Shimo-Ibo Manor|ja|下揖保荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-Ibo Manor|qq=}} (Harima Province), {{ill|Kōzu Manor|ja|居都荘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzu Manor|qq=}} (Bizen Province), and two estates in Bitchū Province: {{ill|Mizuta Manor|ja|水田郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuta Manor|qq=}} and {{ill|Azabu Manor|ja|呰部郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azabu Manor|qq=}}{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. After Emperor Go-Shirakawa passed away in 1190 (Kenkyū 1), Norishige constructed the {{ill|Mikagedō|ja|御影堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikagedō|qq=}} shrine near the emperor’s residence in Yamashina and enshrined his likeness there. The Yamashina family continued to oversee this shrine for generations{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. From the time of {{ill|Yamashina Norimoto|ja|山科教言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norimoto|qq=}} in the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]], the Yamashina family became the hereditary holders of the {{ill|Kurō-donari|ja|内蔵寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurō-donari|qq=}} (Chief of the Imperial Treasury). This position gave them control over the emperor’s attire and ceremonial garments, as well as responsibilities for provisioning court feasts and supervising the supply staff. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzoku|qq=}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emon-dō|qq=}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō family|qq=}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in [[:en:Gagaku|Gagaku]], particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established [[:en:Kazoku|Kazoku]] peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|Haihan-chiken]], the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinroku Kōshō|qq=}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, Ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peerage Act (Japan)|qq=}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokinao|qq=}} was granted the title of [[:en:Count|Hakushaku]] (Count) due to his family's history of holding the [[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]] Rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokikazu|qq=}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} {{translated page|ja|山科家|version=103722627|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Baronial Houses]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamashina family]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Yamashina family]] [[en:Yamashina family]] [[sn:amashina family]] [[ja:山科家]]

{{Infobox Japanese clan |Name= Yamashina Clan (山科家) |Crest= Folded Rhombus (折り入り菱) |CrestName= {{ruby|[[Rhombus Crest|Folded Rhombus]]|おりいりびし}} |Origin= [[Northern Fujiwara Clan|藤原北家]] [[Shijō Clan|四条流]] [[Branches|庶流]] |Founder= [[Fujiwara no Sanekuni|藤原実教]] |Type= [[Kuge]] (Court Nobility) ({{ill|Baronial Houses|ja|羽林家|1=Baronial Houses|1=Baronial Houses|2=ja|3=羽林家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baronial Houses|qq=}}, {{ill|Old Houses|ja|旧家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Houses|qq=}}) |Classification= [[Kazoku]] ([[:en:Count|Count]]) |Homeland= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]] |Stronghold= [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province ]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|兵庫県]] |NotableFigures= [[Yamashina Tokitsugu|山科言継]] |Branches= [[Inokuma Clan (Fujiwara)|猪熊家]] (Baronial Houses)

[[Yamauchi Clan|山浦氏]] (Yamauchi Uesugi Clan, Warrior Clan)
[[Sugizani Clan|杉渓家]] (Barony)

Sanenori was the adopted son ({{ill|Yūshi|ja|猶子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūshi|qq=}}) of {{ill|Tokudaiji Kimitada|ja|徳大寺公親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokudaiji Kimitada|qq=}} and became the first head of the family{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=582}}. Additionally, they managed the financial operations of the imperial court, overseeing revenues from imperial estates. It was during this period that the Yamashina family officially became associated with the traditions of {{ill|Shōzoku|ja|装束|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzoku|qq=}} (ceremonial attire) and {{ill|Emon-dō|ja|衣紋道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emon-dō|qq=}} (courtly dress etiquette){{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the Yamashina family, alongside the {{ill|Shijō family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō family|qq=}}, became one of the hereditary heads of the imperial music office and continued their tradition in [[:en:Gagaku|Gagaku]], particularly specializing in the shō instrument{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, much of the family’s landholdings had been seized, leaving them with only Yamashina East Manor as their last remaining estate. However, even this was confiscated by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} during the tenure of {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, leaving the family with only minor financial support derived from taxation on the imperial provisioning staff{{Cite Kotobank |word=Yamashina Family|encyclopedia=Revised World Encyclopedia, Nihon Daihyakka Jiten (Nipponica) |accessdate=2024-06-09}}. Two Yamashina family members from this era, {{ill|Yamashina Norikuni|ja|山科言国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Norikuni|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamashina Tokitsugu|ja|山科言継|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokitsugu|qq=}}, are known for their diaries, *Norikuni Kyōki* and *Tokitsugu Kyōki*, which are valuable historical records{{sfn|Hashimoto Masanobu|2010|p=583}}. === Meiji Era and Beyond === After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Yamashina family, as former nobility, became part of the newly established [[:en:Kazoku|Kazoku]] peerage system on June 17, 1869 (Meiji 2){{sfn|Asami Masao|1994|p=24}}{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|pp=13–14}}. Following the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|Haihan-chiken]], the Yamashina family was granted a stipend of 311 koku of rice{{sfn|Kasumi Kaikan Kazoku Kakei Taisei Editorial Committee|1985|p=21}}. Under the 1876 {{ill|Kinroku Kōshō|ja|金禄公債証書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinroku Kōshō|qq=}}, this stipend was converted into a monetary allowance of 11,271 yen, Ranking 367th among kazoku stipend recipients{{sfn|Ishikawa Kenjirō|1972|p=60}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Peerage Act (Japan)|ja|華族令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Peerage Act (Japan)|qq=}} in 1884, which formalized the five-tier peerage system, {{ill|Yamashina Tokinao|ja|山科言縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokinao|qq=}} was granted the title of [[:en:Count|Hakushaku]] (Count) due to his family's history of holding the [[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]] Rank multiple times{{sfn|Odabe Yūji|2006|p=324}}. In 2018 (Heisei 30), for the first time since the Meiji era, a living succession took place, with {{ill|Yamashina Tokikazu|ja|山科言和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Tokikazu|qq=}} becoming the 29th head of the Yamashina family{{Cite web|date=2018-12-28 |url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20181228/k00/00m/040/013000c |title= Succession of the Yamashina School of Emon-dō |publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] |accessdate=2024-05-03}}. == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Warrior Nobles of the Warring States: The Life and Diaries of Yamashina Tokitsugu|author=Imaya Akira|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=2002}} * {{Cite book|title=The Nobility and Finances of Medieval Japan|author=Sugahara Masako|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|title=Formation of the Kazoku: Honor and Prestige in Meiji Japan|author=Asami Masao|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Kyoto Nobles in the Modern Era: The Legacy of the Yamashina Clan|author=Ogyu Yoshinori|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|title=The Aristocracy: A Portrait of the Kazoku in Modern Japan|author=Odabe Yūji|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|title=New Compendium of Noble Lineages|author=Kasumi Association|publisher=Kasumi Association|year=1996}} {{translated page|ja|山科家|version=103722627|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Baronial Houses]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamashina family]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Yamashina family]] [[en:Yamashina family]] [[sn:amashina family]] [[ja:山科家]] [[Category:山科家]]
